entry.title: Knowledge Graphs - Datasphere vs HANA Cloud
entry.content:
There are some key differences between the graph capabilities in SAP HANA Cloud and the new announcement SAP Datasphere knowledge graph:

1. SAP HANA Cloud has had a powerful graph engine for analyzing complex relationships in business data for a while now. It allows creating graph workspaces from existing tables/views, querying graphs using openCypher, writing custom graph algorithms, and visualizing graphs.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-hana-graph-resources/ba-p/13505490

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oaSQu9laYKE

 

2. Datasphere Creating knowledge graphs manually is very costly, so SAP Datasphere comes to help by automatically pre-populating them using content already available in SAP Datasphere like connections to source systems, tables, foreign keys etc. SAP Datasphere knowledge graph is a new capability announced in March 2024. As data is onboarded and integrated into SAP Datasphere, it automatically creates an ontology representing the relationships in the data, including business context from SAP sources like S/4HANA

caption by SAP. Currently in Preview

 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQrFjthwOWQ&t=169

https://news.sap.com/2024/03/future-of-data-driven-business-new-sap-datasphere-capabilities/

Conclusion

While the SAP HANA Cloud graph is more for custom graph algorithm development and analysis, the SAP Datasphere knowledge graph is an automated capability that provides a rich context for generative AI use cases by capturing complex data relationships and metadata. The two serve different but complementary purposes in the SAP data and analytics portfolio.

Additional information:
https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/how-use-sap-hana-cloud-graph-model-analyze-complex-your-holitschke

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-hana-graph-resources/ba-p/13505490

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/native-hana-vs-sap-datasphere-findings/qaq-p/12689997

https://www.techzine.eu/blogs/data-management/117331/sap-expands-datasphere-with-knowledge-graph-and-compass/

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/sap-datasphere-is-an-alternative-of-sap-hana-modelling-with-more-features/qaq-p/12701049

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/access-sap-hana-cloud-underneath-of-sap-datasphere/ba-p/13569825

https://developers.sap.com/group.hana-aa-graph-overview.html

entry.title: Get Ready for a Data and AI Revolution: What's Hot at SAP SAPPHIRE 2024 - My personal picks!
entry.content:
Why SAP is Focusing on AI

SAP's strategic pivot towards AI is part of a comprehensive plan aimed at positioning the company for future growth. This initiative includes a significant investment in AI technologies to streamline operations and enhance efficiency. The creation of a dedicated AI growth area under the leadership of Dr. Philipp Herzig, SAP’s new Chief Artificial Intelligence Officer, underscores the centrality of AI in SAP’s future strategy. This move is designed to accelerate the development and deployment of AI solutions that can drive significant business value for SAP’s customers​ (SAP News Center)​​. You can find Dr. Herzig's session at SAPPHIRE here.

The Importance of SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere plays a crucial role in this AI-driven landscape. It represents a comprehensive data management solution that enables businesses to manage, integrate, and utilize their data more effectively. SAP Datasphere simplifies complex data environments by creating a unified data fabric, allowing organizations to access and leverage their data seamlessly. This capability is essential for maximizing the value of AI applications, which rely on high-quality, integrated data to function optimally​.

Datasphere's recent innovations, including the introduction of a knowledge graph and enhanced integration features, make it a pivotal tool for businesses looking to harness the power of data and AI. These advancements help companies transform their data into actionable insights, driving better decision-making and operational efficiency.

With close to 130 sessions at SAPPHIRE, the content can be a bit overwhelming. Below I've listed my top 10 sessions you should attend in my view.

Key Sessions on Data and AI

  1. Data in the Age of AI

    • Speakers: Irfan Khan, Dan Yu, Peter Howland
    • Overview: Discover how to maximize data value across SAP and third-party systems, with insights from Hershey's data transformation journey.
    • Session ID: BTP189

  2. Bringing AI from Prototype to Production

    • Speaker: Walter Sun
    • Overview: Learn about the journey of AI from prototype to production, focusing on identifying valuable AI applications and overcoming implementation challenges.
    • Session ID: TRE307

  3. Business Data Fabric: The Future of Data and AI

    • Speaker: Kaiser Larsen
    • Overview: Explore how SAP Datasphere simplifies data landscapes and empowers organizations.
    • Session ID: BTP151v

  4. SAP Datasphere: Looking Forward

    • Speaker: Torsten Ammon
    • Overview: Gain insights into new features and innovations in SAP Datasphere.
    • Session ID: BTP113

  5. Navigating the AI Era with SAP: Insights from the Executive Suite

    • Speakers: Andreas Welsch, Julia White, Michael Nilles, Walter Sun
    • Overview: Exclusive perspectives on the future of AI with SAP Business AI solutions.
    • Session ID: BAI220v

  6. Experience SAP Business AI in Action

    • Speaker: Richard Grandpierre
    • Overview: Demonstration of the Joule copilot and its impact on efficiency and decision-making.
    • Session ID: BAI900v

  7. Managing Your AI Risk: Responsible AI

    • Speaker: Dr. Bettina Laugwitz
    • Overview: Learn about mitigating AI bias and ensuring trust, privacy, and security.
    • Session ID: TRE312

  8. Learn About SAP's Responsible AI Practices

    • Speaker: Dr. Bettina Laugwitz
    • Overview: Understand SAP's commitment to ethical AI innovations.
    • Session ID: BAI206v

  9. Discover Recent and Upcoming AI Innovations for Business Functions

    • Speaker: Kai Muehlbauer
    • Overview: Learn about the Joule copilot AI Foundation and upcoming AI capabilities.
    • Session ID: BAI202

  10. Unlock the Potential of Your Data Assets: Data-Driven Decision-Making

    • Speaker: Frank Gundlich
    • Overview: Leverage technology and innovation to reshape decision-making processes.
    • Session ID: PAR309v

Conclusion

SAP SAPPHIRE 2024 is a great event for those interested in the latest advancements in Data and AI and SAP is going all in. With a diverse array of sessions, attendees will gain valuable insights into how these technologies can transform their businesses. Don't miss the opportunity to explore these sessions and stay ahead in the rapidly evolving landscape of Data and AI.

It's free, go for it.

For more information and to register for the event, visit the SAP SAPPHIRE 2024 event page and the virtual event page

entry.title: Release Navigator for SAP BTP - What's New in Q2 2024
entry.content:
Check out the Release Navigator.

The Release Navigator for SAP BTP continues to evolve and improve to meet the needs of our customers. With the latest update in Q2 2024 in addition to the quarterly maintenance of resources, users can access new product pages and a new consolidated release schedule in a simplified and streamlined interface.
These updates reflect our commitment to continuously improve content coverage and user experience based on your valuable feedback. In this blog post, we will highlight the key enhancements and additions made in Q2 2024.

We collaborated closely with SAP BTP experts to add new pages for the following products and services in the Release Navigator that are available with the Q2 2024 update:

  • SAP Build Code
  • SAP Alert Notification
  • SAP Automation Pilot
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services
  • SAP Cloud Transport Management
  • SAP Content Agent

The new product pages include Foundation and Cross Services and more. Check them out now.

Check out the new consolidated release schedule in the release calendar.

Release schedules are now being communicated more transparently thanks to close collaboration with various SAP BTP teams. Compared to 2023, more than 40 new products and services now announce their release dates via SAP Notes in SAP for Me.

These notes are accessible through the Consolidated Release Schedules for SAP BTP, which can be found in the Release Calendar section of the navigator.

Please note: To access SAP Notes you need to log in to SAP for Me using your Universal ID or S-user ID. Read more about the required authorizations here.  

The SAP Note Consolidated Release Schedules for SAP BTP

We have published a quarterly maintenance update with the Q2 release cycle. Links and resources have been updated according to the latest feature releases. Moving forward, new and retiring content will be marked with a green or red badge. 

Additionally, in Q1 all release navigators received streamlined and consistent UX across solution areas for improved ease of use.

The release navigator after the Q2 update

 

 

*Disclaimer:  The schedule is subject to change without prior notice including, when necessary, delays or incorporating changes in subsequent update windows and not a commitment, promise, or legal obligation to deliver any material, code, or functionality.

Next updates are scheduled on a quarterly basis in alignment with the Harmonized Release Calendar for SAP Cloud Products.

Looking ahead, here's a sneak peek at some of the roadmap items planned for the release navigator:

  • Q3: Additional UX elements will be added and aligned across solution areas.
  • Q4: More products and services will be included in the Release Navigator.

The Readiness@Scale team at SAP Enterprise Support is dedicated to ensuring release readiness across the SAP Enterprise Support portfolio. Our mission is to empower SAP customers to be prepared and ready to adopt and utilize innovations.

If you have any feedback or specific requests for content in the release navigator, please don't hesitate to reach out to us.

And don't forget to check out our team's blogs tagged with Readiness@Scale.

Happy navigating!

Readiness@Scale, SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Three Real-World Stories: Modernizing SAP Business Warehouse to the Cloud
entry.content:
Many SAP BW customers are looking to utilize cloud-based solutions while protecting their previous investments. Although the advantages of cloud modernization are evident, customers require a flexible approach that leverages their current technology and expertise. SAP Datasphere provides that path for BW customers.

We'll explore how three forward-looking organizations across the energy, manufacturing, and healthcare sectors have successfully navigated this transition and the benefits they've realized from it.

 

Modernizing with SAP Datasphere

Snam, an Italian energy infrastructure company, and Kemira, a global leader in sustainable chemical solutions, have both leveraged the robust data management and analytics capabilities of SAP BW to consolidate data across numerous systems, enabling centralized reporting and analytics. They've used these capabilities to support strategic planning and operational efficiency, modeling business scenarios, and developing sustainable business practices. 

When these organizations looked to modernize, their existing SAP BW setup served as a critical stepping stone. SAP BW's robust data governance frameworks ensured data integrity, security, and compliance, crucial for businesses in highly regulated industries.

However, the motivation for modernizing isn't just about new technologies. Legacy systems often result in a complex patchwork of instances and integrations, making data management a disjointed process. By modernizing to SAP Datasphere, these companies built upon their previous achievements and addressed new demands for greater agility, scalability, and innovation in a cloud-centric world.

  • Snam's transition to SAP Datasphere revolutionized its strategic planning and reporting approach. It gave business users the autonomy to adapt quickly to new challenges, reducing reliance on IT and enabling a significant leap in operational agility.
  • Kemira experienced transformative power by integrating SAP Datasphere with existing systems, streamlining processes, and enabling deeper data-driven insights. This transition allowed them to harness data more effectively, boosting efficiency and performance.
  • Nipro, a leading international healthcare entity, understood that migration required business continuity. The bridge between SAP Datasphere and SAP BW helped connect Nipro’s legacy systems to its new SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition solution. This transition resulted in more precise business insights delivered in real time.

 

Evolve your SAP Business Warehouse

 Modernizing your BW environment can unlock more business use cases, following in the footsteps of these forward-thinking companies. This typically involves comprehensive planning, handling customizations and integrations, launching extensive change management, starting with targeted pilot projects, appointing data owners for governance standards, and continuous evaluation and iterative improvements. With an integrated approach that radically simplifies your data landscape, SAP Datasphere enables a business data fabric architecture, providing smooth and scalable access to data without duplication.

Read about the SAP BW transformation stories.

 

Next Steps

Whether you’re starting fresh or modernizing an existing system, having a solid plan is key for a successful move to the cloud.

SAP is now offering a limited-time, complimentary SAP BW system assessment to help you optimize your transition to SAP Datasphere. Through this assessment, you can gain insight into your SAP BW environment and learn which objects are active and frequently used and whether they can be modernized. 

The assessment helps deliver a reliable basis for an actionable modernization plan. With the right information, you can modernize on your terms. Get started today by applying for a complimentary assessment.

entry.title: Connecting SAP Analytics Cloud to Power BI Using Azure Data Factory
entry.content:
Introduction

Many companies are using SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for planning alongside Power BI for reporting. However, users often request to utilize SAC planning data within Power BI. This integration can be challenging due to differences in data handling and capabilities. If you are facing similar demands, our blog provides a practical solution to this issue.

In this blog post, we will guide you through creating an export service for SAC that integrates seamlessly with Power BI using Azure Data Factory (ADF). This straightforward setup leverages the SAC Data Export Service API, ADF and Multi-Action API Step to ensure minimal compute usage and cost efficiency.

To demonstrate how this setup works in practice, we've created a demo video. Watch the video below to see how updating data in an SAC story seamlessly updates the data in Power BI. This will give you a clear understanding of the real-time integration between SAC and Power BI using ADF.

Prerequisites

Before we begin, ensure you meet the following prerequisites

  • SAC Tenant, including Planning Model and related story
  • Azure Data Factory Account
  • Azure Storage Account in ADLS
  • Power BI

Step-by-Step Guide

  1. Creating the Data Export Pipeline in ADF
  2. Triggering ADF Pipeline through Multi-Action API Step
  3. Connecting Power BI with Azure Data Lake Storage
  4. Running the entire process

Creating the Data Export Pipeline

The pipeline consists of four main parts:

  1. REST Service: Create a REST service for the desired model.
  2. Copy Table Names: Identifies all endpoints for the specified model.
  3. Lookup Tables: Outputs the names obtained from "Copy Table Names".
  4. ForEach Loop: Uses the SAC REST API to retrieve data from the endpoints and copy it to ADLS.

To help you visualize the above-mentioned steps, we've created a short GIF that quickly demonstrates the process.

 Setting up REST Service

  1. Create a REST Service:
    1. Set up a REST Service with base URL:
      {your SAC tenant}/api/v1/dataexport/providers/sac/{model ID}

    2. Choose "OAuth 2.0 Client Credential" for the authentications type. We will name this service "REST_EXPORT_API_DEMO".

  2. Generate OAuth Credentials:
    1. In your SAC tenant, navigate to 'System > Administration > App Integration' to find your token endpoint and create a client to get the client ID and client secret.

 Setting up "Copy Table Names"

  1. Create a REST Dataset:
    1. Set up a REST dataset using the linked service "REST_EXPORT_API_DEMO".
      Name this table "Tables".

  2. Data Source Configuration:
    1. In "Copy Table Names" select the REST dataset you just created.

       

    2. Create a CSV file to store the table names. The file path should be:
      {Storage account}/{model ID}/Table_names.csv
      This will be used as the sink for "Copy Table Names".

       

    3. Use the just created CSV file as the sink dataset.

       

    4. Ensure that the mapping includes only the table names.

       

Setting up "Lookup Tables"

Use the sink dataset from the "Copy Table Names" step as the source for "Lookup Tables".

 

 

 

Configuring the "ForEach" Loop

Under settings, set the "Items" to: @activity('Lookup tables').output.value

 

 

This configuration ensures the loop iterates over all table names, using them in the subsequent copy data activities within the loop.

Configuring "Copy Data" within the "ForEach" loop

  1. Set up a new REST Dataset: This will be called "REST_API_ForEach". We will use the "REST_EXPORT_API_DEMO" as linked service, but will now add a dynamic relative URL.

     

     

     

  2. Setting up Source: We will use the above dataset as source and the item setting that configured under "Configuring the "ForEach" Loop". This way we will access the endpoint for each table in the given model.

     

  3. Creating a dynamic JSON sink dataset: The JSON sink dataset is created such that we will have one unique JSON file for each table in the model, which will inherit its name from the model.

     

     

     

     

Setting the sink in "Copy Data": We now simply use the above created table along with the "Items" configured under "Configuring the "ForEach" Loop".

 

The ADF pipeline is now fully configured and should export the model from SAC into the Azure blob storage, under the path defined in the first step of  "Creating a dynamic JSON sink dataset".

Triggering ADF Pipeline through Multi-Action

Register an App in Azure

  1. Go to 'Home > App Registration > Endpoint' and copy the Token URL:
    "OAuth 2.0 token endpoint (v2)".
  2. Go to 'Home > App Registration' and click '+ New Registration'.
  3. Name your app and select 'Accounts in this organizational directory only (Single tenant)'.
  4. Copy the 'Application (client) ID', this is your "OAuth Client ID".

Configure OAuth Credentials

  1. Go to 'Certificates & Secrets' and create a new client secret. Copy the 'Value' as your client secret.

     

  2. Ensure that the created App, "SAC", has the permission 'user_impersonation'.

     

  3. Assign the app the role of 'Data Factory Contributor':
    'Azure Portal > Data factories > Your Data Factory > Access Control (IAM) > Add Role Assignment'.

Create the Connection in SAC

Create a connection using the OAuth Client ID, Secret and OAuth 2.0 token endpoint (v2) retrieved in the previous steps.

The OAuth client ID is a unique, autogenerated ID that identifies your Azure application to SAP Cloud Platform and verifies its authorization to access requested resources. The specific OAuth client ID used above is given as the "Application ID" retrieved in the last step of "Register an App in Azure".

The secret is a confidential password that is associated with the client application. It is used by SAC to prove its identity to the Azure platform. It is the 'Value' retrieved in the first step of "Configure OAuth Credentials".

The "Token URL" is the endpoint that SAC will use to request an access token. The access token is a temporary authorization that is used by the client application to access the requested data or services.

Creating the Multi-Action

  • Add a data action: to publish changes made to the story before exporting.

Configure the export API step: To do this, choose the connection made earlier and use the API URL:

The URL is given by:
https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{Subscription   ID}/resourceGroups/{Resource Group Name}/providers/Microsoft.DataFactory/factories/{Data Factory ID}/pipelines/{Pipeline ID}/createRun?api-version=2018-06-01  

Secondly, choose the request method "Synchronous Return".

  • Add the Multi-Action to a story which is uses the same model as the one in ADF.

Connecting Power BI with ADLS

Retrieve the Endpoint of the ADLS

  • Go to your storage account in Azure where the tables are saved and search for endpoints.
  • Copy the Data Lake Storage endpoint (not the resource ID).

Configure Power BI

  • In Power BI, click 'Get Data' and select 'Azure Data Lake Storage Gen 2'.
  • Paste the endpoint and add the path to the folder:
    https://{storageaccount}.dfs.core.windows.net/{Blob container}/{model_id}

Unpack Data

For each table, unpack the data. Repeat this process for all tables.





Running the entire process

  1. Modify the Story in SAC: Make changes and trigger the multi-action.
  2. Monitor ADF: Go to ADF and ensure the pipeline is triggered and completed successfully.
  3. Refresh Power BI Data: Refresh the data in Power BI to reflect the changes.

By following these steps, you can efficiently export data from SAC to Power BI using ADF, ensuring a seamless and cost-effective integration.

entry.title: Creating a story with Dynamic Version & Date Combinations
entry.content:
Introduction

 

Designing a story in SAP Analytics Cloud can be easy and straightforward with many features to create calculations, columns, styles. The drag and drop functionality helps users create reports with different layouts of various complexity . Optimized story design is also helpful to create advanced stories and reporting layouts using JavaScript to achieve complex functionality and user interactions.

When designing any report or input form, if the layout contains Version and Date dimensions, adding multiple members of versions and Months or Years can result in a Layout that has columns with all the combinations of the members. For example, if the selections contain Actual, Budget for Version and Date contains years 2024 and 2025, the report will have 4 columns. Even though the intention is to show only 2 columns with combinations of [Actual, 2023] and [Budget, 2024], the final report contains all 4 combinations.

There are ways in which we can show only valid/required combinations, for example hiding unwanted columns or suppress columns with no data or using Advanced Filters. But these approaches are not dynamic. Every time there is a change in Version or Date, the columns need to be adjusted manually. It might also create many static reports to achieve different combination reports.

The alternate approach is to create dynamic reports is using Optimized story design using JavaScript and SAC Advanced Script API to show columns with only valid/required combinations. This blog provides required design and logic required for interactions and filtering.
 

Step 1 - Creating Design Elements

The design of the dashboard uses dropdown menus instead of filters define within Story. The user can select required Dimension members from dropdown and on submit the selections will be sent to the table filters dynamically. We will use story calculations to create required combinations and further use SAC Analytics designer and API to update dimension filters with required combinations.

Step 1-a
  • Create a New story using Optimized Story design.
  • Add 4 Dropdown menus ( Insert -> Filters/Controls -> Dropdown). 2 for Actual / 2023 and 2 for Budget/ 2024. 
  • Dropdown names dd_fy1, dd_fy2 for years & dd_cat1, dd_cat2 for versions. 
  • Add 1 submit button ( Insert -> Button )
  • Button name : btn_1
  • Add 1 Table and connect to model. Add necessary filters. ( We need to update the filters at a later stage to show necessary columns)
  • Table Name: table_1

 

Step 1-b
Once the dropdown menus are added, from the script edit page, add the values required for the dropdown menus. Year dropdown has values as Years and Version dropdown has values as Version names. Below images show the options used for each dropdown.
 
 
 
 
The final result will be 4 dropdown menus with values and a Submit button. 
 

 

Step 2 - Logic 

Till now we have added design elements in the Story. Now we need to add logic to capture the values selected from dropdown menus and send them story filters.

logic to be added on btn_1 onClick function

The logic has 3 main parts. 

  1. extract the selections used from the dropdown menus and pass them to variables and arrays
  2. create valid combinations by combining Version + year values and push into array
  3. Filter the Version, Year and Valid Combinations values on the table

 

 

 

 

//extract selected versions into variables 
var version1 = dd_cat1.getSelectedKey(); 
var version2 = dd_cat2.getSelectedKey(); 

// append public to variables created above 
var col1_version = "public."+version1; 
var col2_version = "public."+version2; 

//extract selected versions into variables 
var col1_fy = dd_fy1.getSelectedKey(); 
var col2_fy = dd_fy2.getSelectedKey(); 

// save table into variable 
var ds_table1 = table_1.getDataSource(); 

// get dimension names into variables. Note the order of dimensions added in columns. 
// 0 is version, 1 is for year 
var version_col =table_1.getDimensionsOnColumns()[0]; 
var fy_col =table_1.getDimensionsOnColumns()[1]; 

// create an empty array to hold version values and add the version variables created above 
var version_cols_values = ArrayUtils.create(Type.string); 
version_cols_values.push(col1_version); 
version_cols_values.push(col2_version); 

// create an empty array to hold year values and add the year variables created above 
var fy_cols_values = ArrayUtils.create(Type.string); 
fy_cols_values.push(col1_fy); 
fy_cols_values.push(col2_fy); 

// create an empty array for valid combinations 
// for example Actual2023, Budget2024 
var valid_comb = ArrayUtils.create(Type.string); 
valid_comb.push(version1+col1_fy); 
valid_comb.push(version2+col2_fy); 


// set the 3 dimension filters for Version, Date, valid_combinations on the table 
ds_table1.setDimensionFilter(version_col,version_cols_values); 
ds_table1.setDimensionFilter(fy_col,fy_cols_values); 

ds_table1.setDimensionFilter(table_1.getDimensionsOnColumns()[2],valid_comb);

 

 

 

 

Step 3 - Story

The JavaScript logic above filters the dimension values for Version and Time , Fiscal Year values. But filtering alone on Version and Time still result in additional columns which are not required. So we need to create an additional calculated dimension call "valid_combinations" to identify the required columns and filter them during the runtime.

 

 

Below is the code used for creating the calculated dimension. The code uses Version and Fiscal Year to derive the all the possible combinations of columns. One can add many versions, years and create all past and future values possible.

 

 

 

 

IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Actual" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2022"),
        "Actual2022",
        IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Actual" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2023"),
                "Actual2023",
                IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Actual" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2024"),
                        "Actual2024",
                        IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Actual" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2025"),
                                "Actual2025",
                                IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Budget" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2022"),
                                        "Budget2022",
                                        IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Budget" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2023"),
                                                "Budget2023",
                                                IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Budget" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2024"),
                                                        "Budget2024",
                                                        IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Budget" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2025"),
                                                                "Budget2025",
                                                                IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Plan" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2022"),
                                                                        "Plan2022",
                                                                        IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Plan" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2023"),
                                                                                "Plan2023",
                                                                                IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Plan" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2024"),
                                                                                        "Plan2024",
                                                                                        IF(([d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Version].[p/ID]="public.Plan" and [d/"PLANNING_MODEL_SAC":Time].[p/FISCAL_YEAR]="2025"),
                                                                                                "Plan2025",
                                                                                                "Actual2025"
                                                                                        )
                                                                                )
                                                                        )
                                                                )
                                                        )
                                                )
                                        )
                                )
                        )
                )
        )
)

 

 

 

 

Result

The final result will contain table with only required columns.

entry.title: Importance of Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Accurate currency conversion ensures that financial statements reflect true value, enabling better decision-making and compliance with regulatory requirements. Without effective currency conversion, businesses may face issues such as financial discrepancies, inaccurate forecasting, and challenges in performance evaluation across different regions.

"Twenty-four-hour global nature of currency markets, exchange rates are constantly shifting from day to day and even from minute to minute, sometimes in small increments and sometimes quite dramatically" – Harvard Business Services.

In an interconnected world where businesses operate across borders, efficient currency conversion is essential. Currency conversion significantly impacts reporting and business analytics in the following ways:

Translation of Financial Statements:

Multinational corporations translate foreign incomes, expenses, assets, and liabilities into their reporting currency using relevant exchange rates.

Variances in Reported Financials:

Exchange rate fluctuations can result in significant variances in reported financials.

Currency conversion in SAP involves converting monetary values from one currency to another based on predefined exchange rates. This is important for multinational companies and businesses engaged in cross-border transactions.

Exchange rates in SAP define how one currency is converted to another. Let’s explore some significant business use cases where reporting with currency conversion is extensively required:

Case 1: Global Operations and Multinational Businesses

Companies operating in multiple countries need to manage different currencies. Currency conversion allows them to integrate financial data across various locations and ensure accurate financial reporting.

Case 2: Consolidated Financial Statements

Currency conversion in SAP enables the creation of consolidated financial reports in a single group currency.

Case 3: Budgeting and Forecasting

Companies often need to budget and forecast in a specific currency while dealing with costs and revenues in other currencies. Currency conversion allows for accurate planning and forecasting, providing a unified view of the organization’s financial health.

Currency conversion is used when posting financial information where the reporting currency differs from the transaction currency.

Currency Conversion Methods In SAP Datasphere

In an increasingly globalized business environment, companies often deal with transactions and data from multiple countries, involving various currencies. This complexity makes accurate currency conversion a critical aspect of financial reporting, budgeting, and analytics. SAP Datasphere, with its robust currency conversion capabilities, ensures businesses maintain financial accuracy and consistency across their operations.

Steps to Create Currency Conversion

Use Case

Step 01:

The client needs a report in Indian currency, but we have data in Datasphere in USD. So, using the currency conversion, we are converting from USD to INR.

Step 02:

 

  • Check whether the connection of the source is in an active state.
    • Confirm access to Data flows, Tables, and Views, because running the data flow loads data into the local table before it becomes available in the views.

     

    • To perform currency translation in SAP Datasphere, the following tables must be available in your space:

     

    1. TCURV - Exchange rate types
    2. TCURW - Exchange rate type text
    3. TCURX - Decimal places in currencies
    4. TCURN - Quotations
    5. TCURR - Exchange rates
    6. TCURF - Conversion factors
    7. TCURC - Currency codes
    8. TCURT - Currency text

     

      
  • DataFlows

 

  • Tables

 

  • Views

 

Step 03:
  • In this case we don’t have data in the target table, so we have to run the data flow to load the data to local table.

Step 04:
  • Here, we are converting the currency from USD to INR.
  • We have filtered “From Currency” as “USD” and “To Currency” as “INR” to obtain the exchange rate type.
  • After that, obtain exchange rate types M & P for the scenario.

Step 05:
  • For this scenario we have selected the source measure as “Gross Amount,” so we have to change the “Semantic Type” to “Amount with Currency” and “Unit Column” can be selected accordingly.

Step 06:
  • We have selected the billing document date as the transaction date because we are using the billing document fact model.

Step 07:
  • The conversion from USD to INR is now complete.

Best Practices for Currency Conversion

  • Address rounding and precision issues:

              Rounding Rules: Apply consistent rounding rules to avoid discrepancies in financial reports.

               Precision: Ensure that rounding practices maintain accuracy, especially withlarge datasets.

  • Maintain consistency and accuracy:

              Accurate Data Entry: Ensure accurate entry of financial data and exchange rates to minimize errors during currency conversion.

               Data Quality Checks: Regularly perform data quality checks to identify and rectify inaccuracies in exchange rates and financial data.

  • Regular updates and monitoring of exchange rates:
     

                Periodic Reviews: Conduct regular reviews of your currency conversion processes and update procedures as necessary to adapt to changing financial environments.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in June
entry.content:
SAPPHIRE is in the books now, and we did not stop to deliver great 📢 new features in SAP Datasphere. Feel free to watch SAPPHIRE session replays on-demand on the virtual event platform.

There are also lots of excellent new blogs in the community, a new bite sized video series about the data marketplace, and new Customer Engagement Initiative for SAP Datasphere where you can participate.

My top features in June

Column level lineage

The impact and lineage analysis is now enabled to get one level deeper and allows you to select one or more columns in your analyzed object and show their sources and the columns that they impact. More information.

 

Many improvements in the data integration area

There are multiple improvements in the data integration and monitoring area.

Transformation Flows
You can now add a view as well as open SQL schema objects as a source in a transformation flow. More information.

Task Chains
You can now include Open SQL procedures within task chains (more information). As a prerequisite an administrator must grant EXECUTE privileges for objects in the Open SQL schema space. More information.

Replication Flows
You can now add objects to a replication flow in status Active without having to stop the run first. More information. Moreover, you can now specify a thread count greater than 1 in replication flows for delta load and thus enable parallel processing. More information.

Monitoring
Multiple improvements also in the data integration monitoring, where you can now also see local tables which have been created in a space and check the metrics related to these tables. More information. 
The status text descriptions in the replication flow monitoring was rephrased so that they are more self-explanatory. They now differentiate more clearly between the situation at flow level and at object level. More information.
And last but not least the details screen in the Views monitor now indicates if your view data is protected by a data access control. More information.

 

 

Data Marketplace Video Series

There is an excellent video series 🎥 created by my colleague @FlorianN describing and showing all facets of the SAP Datasphere data marketplace 🛒 in a few minutes. Use them to learn more about the data marketplace in general and the different components it consists of.
👉New video series for SAP Datasphere data marketplace

 

SAP Customer Engagement Initiative

The second cycle of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative in 2024 has officially kicked off in June, bringing with it 51 new projects to fuel innovation and collaboration. As part of that initiative, you can now signup for the ➡️ User Validation for new SAP Datasphere homepage until July 11th 2024. You will find all details on the project page and also register yourself there for participation.
👉Check out the other projects.

 

My top blogs in June

My favorite blog in June is highlighting the newly introduced validation framework in SAP Datasphere. The first checks provide initial validation rules to check the consistency of your view key columns and hierarchy nodes and provide detailed output about the erroneous records. @senthurshree describes the framework and its current test very well in her blog with more validations to come in the next quarters
➡️ SAP Datasphere Data Validations

The next blog I would like to highlight is also about authorizations from SAP S/4HANA written by @JensBraun 
➡️ Integrate SAP S/4HANA authorizations into SAP Datasphere

A very nice blog is covering @Wolfgang_Eptings blog about the role of Data Products with
➡️ 10+ ways to reshape your SAP landscape with SAP Business Technology Platform – Blog 8

My last blog highlight of June written by @heiko_schneider about new DAC types
➡️ Introducing new type of Data Access Control "Operator and Value" for SAP Datasphere

 

More blogs to check out 👇

  • SAP Datasphere Data Validations
  • SAP Datasphere, SAP BW bridge learning journey
  • SAP Datasphere - BW ODP Delta
  • New certification available! SAP Data Engineer - Data Fabric for SAP BW/4HANA and SAP Datasphere
  • Setting up Trial SAP Datasphere Account with Trial SAP SAC Live Connection
  • Datasphere - Replication Flow : Enabling the ABAP CDS Extractor
  • Three Real-World Stories: Modernizing SAP Business Warehouse to the Cloud
  • GitHub sample for working with SAP Datasphere and SAP Data Quality Management, microservices
  • Create Data Products with SAP Datasphere Marketplace
  • Data Access Control in SAP Datasphere - Hierarchy DAC
  • What's New in Q2 2024 for the Release Navigator for SAP BTP
  • New video series for SAP Datasphere data marketplace
  • Introducing new type of Data Access Control "Operator and Value" for SAP Datasphere
  • Shape the Future of SAP Solutions: Join the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative 2024 Cycle 2
  • SAP Schulungskonferenzen: Expertenwissen aus erster Hand!

  • New SAP Analytics Cloud Performance Workshop

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Getting Started, Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting and the FAQ for SAP Datasphere.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.
